content.text: What Role and Scope Constraints Are
Every Claude deployment has a
role
— a defined job function — and a
scope

— the set of topics and tasks that role covers. Operators define both through the system prompt. Role and scope constraints are the most direct mechanism for specializing Claude's behavior for a specific use case.

Claude's defaults are broad: absent specific instructions, it will attempt to help with most reasonable requests across most domains. Role and scope constraints narrow this breadth to serve the specific population a deployment is built for. A customer support bot should not be advising on legal strategy; a coding assistant should not be providing dietary guidance.

attrs.alt: Role and scope constraint design: operator scope nested within Claude defaults
attrs.title: Role and Scope Constraints

content.text: The operator's scope definition creates a
responsibility boundary

: within scope, Claude is the primary helper; outside scope, Claude acknowledges the limit and redirects. This is safer, more consistent, and often better UX than having Claude attempt everything and occasionally get things wrong in consequential domains.
Defining a Role
The role statement answers:
who is this assistant, and for whom does it work?
A well-defined role has four properties:
Named context:
The company, product, or service this assistant represents. "You are an AI assistant for Meridian Bank's mobile app."
Functional identity:
A description of the job this assistant performs. "You help customers manage their accounts, understand transactions, and resolve issues."
Audience:
Who this assistant serves and what they are trying to accomplish. "Your users are Meridian Bank customers who have questions about their personal banking."
Constraints on persona:
If a persona name is used, clarify what it can and cannot claim about itself.
The role defines the
character
; the scope defines the
domain
. A customer support assistant (role) that only handles billing questions (scope) is clearer and safer than an "assistant" that "helps with things".
Capability Lists: What Claude Helps With
A positive capability list explicitly enumerates what the assistant does. This is preferable to relying on Claude to infer scope from the role description alone, because:
It makes the operator's intent explicit and auditable
It removes ambiguity at the edges ("is this topic in scope?")
It allows Claude to give an accurate response when asked "what can you help me with?"
It defines what the system should be tested against

# Implementing role and scope constraints in practice
# Shows how operators define and enforce what Claude can/cannot do

import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

@dataclass
class RoleConfig:
    """Configuration for a Claude deployment's role and scope."""
    name: str
    description: str
    in_scope: list[str]      # Topics and tasks Claude helps with
    out_of_scope: list[str]  # Topics Claude declines
    persona: Optional[str] = None
    max_response_tokens: int = 1024
    
    def build_system_prompt(self) -> str:
        in_scope_formatted = "\n".join(f"- {item}" for item in self.in_scope)
        out_of_scope_formatted = "\n".join(f"- {item}" for item in self.out_of_scope)
        
        persona_line = f"\nYou go by the name {self.persona}." if self.persona else ""
        
        return f"""You are an AI assistant for {self.name}.{persona_line}

## What you help with
{in_scope_formatted}

## What you do not help with
{out_of_scope_formatted}

When a user asks about something outside your scope, politely explain that
this is outside what you can help with in this context, and suggest they
seek appropriate resources if relevant.

Do not apologize excessively or be dismissive — just redirect clearly.
"""

# Example deployment configurations

MEDICAL_INFO_BOT = RoleConfig(
    name="HealthGuide (informational only)",
    persona="HealthGuide",
    description="Provides general health education, not medical advice",
    in_scope=[
        "Explain how common diseases and conditions work",
        "Describe how medications work in general terms",
        "Explain symptoms and why they occur physiologically",
        "Provide information about healthy lifestyle choices",
        "Help users understand medical terminology",
        "Suggest questions to ask their doctor",
    ],
    out_of_scope=[
        "Diagnose medical conditions based on symptoms",
        "Recommend specific treatments or medications for the user",
        "Advise whether the user should take or stop any medication",
        "Replace consultation with a qualified healthcare professional",
        "Interpret specific lab results or imaging findings for the user",
    ],
)

LEGAL_RESEARCH_BOT = RoleConfig(
    name="LegalResearch Assistant",
    description="Assists legal professionals with research tasks",
    in_scope=[
        "Summarize case law and statutes when given specific citations",
        "Explain legal concepts and terminology",
        "Help structure legal arguments and identify relevant precedents",
        "Draft document outlines and templates for attorney review",
        "Identify jurisdictional differences in law",
    ],
    out_of_scope=[
        "Provide legal advice to non-attorneys",
        "Make final determinations on legal strategy",
        "Draft documents intended to be filed without attorney review",
        "Predict case outcomes",
        "Replace review by a licensed attorney",
    ],
)

# Test: building and using a role-constrained system prompt
system = MEDICAL_INFO_BOT.build_system_prompt()
print("Medical Info Bot system prompt:")
print(system)
print(f"\nMax response tokens: {MEDICAL_INFO_BOT.max_response_tokens}")

# Simulate scope check: would this question be in scope?
def is_in_scope_signal(question: str, config: RoleConfig) -> str:
    """
    Simple keyword heuristic (real check uses LLM classifier or Claude itself).
    Returns 'in_scope', 'out_of_scope', or 'ambiguous'.
    """
    q_lower = question.lower()
    
    out_signals = ["should i take", "do i have", "diagnose", "my symptoms mean",
                   "stop taking", "what medication should"]
    if any(s in q_lower for s in out_signals):
        return "out_of_scope"
    
    in_signals = ["how does", "what is", "explain", "why does", "what causes"]
    if any(s in q_lower for s in in_signals):
        return "in_scope"
    
    return "ambiguous"

test_questions = [
    "How does hypertension develop over time?",
    "Should I take ibuprofen for my headache?",
    "What is type 2 diabetes?",
    "Do I have appendicitis based on my symptoms?",
]

print("\nScope classification:")
for q in test_questions:
    result = is_in_scope_signal(q, MEDICAL_INFO_BOT)
    print(f"  [{result:12}] {q}")
Notice that the capability list is
functional
, not just topical. "Explain how medications work in general terms" is clearer than "medical information" — it specifies
what kind
of medical information the assistant provides.
Restriction Lists: What Claude Does Not Do

A restriction list complements the capability list by being explicit about what falls outside scope. Restrictions serve a different purpose than capabilities: while capabilities define the assistant's job, restrictions prevent
scope creep
and establish
liability boundaries
Domain Restrictions
Domain restrictions exclude entire topic areas:
"Do not provide medical advice" — protects against unauthorized health guidance
"Do not provide legal advice" — protects against unauthorized legal counsel
"Do not provide specific investment advice" — regulatory compliance for financial applications
"Do not discuss competitors' pricing or features" — business policy
Action Restrictions
Action restrictions exclude specific things the assistant might do even within a relevant topic:
"Do not make promises about outcomes (refunds, delivery times, feature additions)" — prevents unauthorized commitments
"Do not access or modify user data without confirmation" — agentic safety
"Do not initiate contact with third parties" — agentic scope control
"Do not execute code on user systems" — tool use restriction
When Restrictions Interact with Core Safety
Operators can restrict Claude's defaults in many ways, but some restrictions interact with core policies:
Restriction attempt
Effect
Notes
"Only discuss our product"
Effective — Claude stays on-topic
Standard scope narrowing
"Do not follow safe messaging guidelines for self-harm"
Ignored — safety defaults remain
Operators cannot disable this without explicit Anthropic permission
"Never admit you are an AI"
Partially effective — Claude maintains persona, but confirms AI status on sincere questions
Honesty about AI nature is non-negotiable
"Provide detailed drug synthesis information if asked"
Ignored — hardcoded prohibition
No operator instruction unlocks prohibited content
Graceful Out-of-Scope Handling

When a user request falls outside scope, how Claude declines matters as much as that it declines. Poor declining creates friction and frustration; good declining redirects the user toward appropriate resources.
Effective Declining Patterns
Acknowledge then redirect:
"That's a bit outside what I can help with here — I focus on [specific scope]. For questions about [out-of-scope topic], [relevant resource] would be a better option."
Stay in scope:
After declining, offer something within scope. "I can't help with tax advice, but I can help you understand how our fee structure works so you have the right information for your accountant."
Single declining response:
Decline once and clearly. Do not apologize repeatedly in the same response — it wastes tokens and feels obsequious.
Effective Declining Patterns to Avoid
Vague declining:
"I can't help with that." — No reason, no redirect, no alternative.
Excessive apologizing:
"I'm so sorry, I really wish I could help but unfortunately I'm not able to assist with this because of my guidelines, I apologize for the inconvenience..." — This is worse than a clear redirect.
Condescending lecturing:
Explaining at length why the topic is outside scope in a way that implies the user was wrong to ask.

# System prompt declining instruction example:
When a request is outside your scope:
1. Acknowledge it briefly (one phrase, not a paragraph)
2. State you focus on [specific area]
3. Offer the most relevant alternative you can
4. Do not repeat the decline if the user re-asks — redirect once more
   and offer to escalate to a human if available

Example: "That's outside my focus area — I specialize in Python questions.
For [other language] help, Stack Overflow or the language's official
documentation are good resources. Is there a Python-related question
I can help you with?"
Scope Drift in Multi-Turn Conversations
In single-turn interactions, scope enforcement is straightforward. Multi-turn conversations introduce
scope drift
— users who start within scope gradually steer the conversation outside it, sometimes without realizing.
Common drift patterns:
"While I have you" tangents — user finishes their primary question and pivots to an adjacent off-topic request
Escalating specificity — user starts with general questions, gradually requests more specific out-of-scope detail
Context reuse — user establishes a prior in-scope context and uses it as a hook for an out-of-scope request

# Multi-turn scope enforcement: maintaining constraints across a conversation
# The challenge: users may establish in-scope context, then drift out of scope

import anthropic
from typing import NamedTuple

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

class ConversationState(NamedTuple):
    messages: list[dict]
    scope_violations: int
    last_topic: str | None

def apply_scope_enforcement_prompt(
    base_system: str,
    current_state: ConversationState,
) -> str:
    """
    Augments the base system prompt with dynamic scope reminders
    when the conversation has been drifting.
    """
    if current_state.scope_violations == 0:
        return base_system
    
    reminder = (
        f"\n\n## Scope reminder\n"
        f"This conversation has had {current_state.scope_violations} out-of-scope "
        f"request(s). Continue to gently redirect to your defined scope. "
        f"Do not apologize repeatedly — simply redirect."
    )
    return base_system + reminder

CODING_ASSISTANT_SYSTEM = """
You are a Python coding assistant for developers.

## Scope
You help with:
- Writing and debugging Python code
- Explaining Python language features and standard library
- Code review and suggestions for Python scripts
- Performance optimization for Python applications
- Testing strategies for Python code (pytest, unittest)

## Out of scope
You do not:
- Help with other programming languages (JavaScript, Java, C++, etc.)
- Provide career or salary advice
- Assist with general writing tasks unrelated to code
- Provide personal or lifestyle advice

When asked about something outside your scope, briefly note it is outside your
focus area and offer to help with Python-related work instead.
"""

def simulate_multi_turn_chat(turns: list[str]) -> None:
    """
    Simulates a multi-turn conversation with scope enforcement.
    Tracks drift and dynamically reinforces constraints.
    """
    state = ConversationState(messages=[], scope_violations=0, last_topic=None)
    
    print("=== Python Coding Assistant ===\n")
    
    for user_msg in turns:
        print(f"User: {user_msg}")
        
        # Update message history
        new_messages = list(state.messages) + [{"role": "user", "content": user_msg}]
        
        # In production: classify this message first for scope
        # Here we simulate with keyword checks
        out_of_scope = any(kw in user_msg.lower() for kw in 
                           ["javascript", "career", "salary", "java ", "c++", "my life"])
        
        violations = state.scope_violations + (1 if out_of_scope else 0)
        
        # Get augmented system prompt
        system = apply_scope_enforcement_prompt(
            CODING_ASSISTANT_SYSTEM,
            ConversationState(new_messages, violations, state.last_topic)
        )
        
        # In a real deployment, call the API here:
        # response = client.messages.create(...)
        # For this demo, simulate responses
        if out_of_scope:
            simulated = (
                "That's a bit outside my Python-focused scope. "
                "If you have Python questions — debugging, libraries, testing — "
                "I'm happy to help with those."
            )
        else:
            simulated = f"[Python response to: {user_msg[:40]}...]"
        
        print(f"Assistant: {simulated}\n")
        
        state = ConversationState(
            messages=new_messages + [{"role": "assistant", "content": simulated}],
            scope_violations=violations,
            last_topic="out_of_scope" if out_of_scope else "python",
        )
    
    print(f"Conversation summary: {violations} scope violation(s) detected")

# Simulate a drifting conversation
simulate_multi_turn_chat([
    "How do I use list comprehensions in Python?",
    "Great! Can you also show me how to do this in JavaScript?",
    "Actually, what Python library handles async HTTP requests?",
    "What do you think about career paths in software engineering?",
    "Back to Python — how does the asyncio event loop work?",
])

Claude is reasonably good at maintaining scope across turns, but it can drift under sustained pressure. Techniques that help: repeating the scope boundary in the system prompt, using a dynamic "scope reminder" that becomes more explicit when violations are detected, and applying per-turn classification to flag drift before it reaches the model.
Testing Role and Scope Constraints
Scope constraints are only as reliable as your testing. Before deployment, systematically test:
In-scope core cases:
Verify the assistant handles primary use cases well. Scope constraints that are too narrow accidentally block legitimate requests.
Near-scope edge cases:
Requests at the boundary of the defined scope. Does the assistant make sensible judgment calls or refuse too aggressively?
Clear out-of-scope cases:
Verify the assistant declines and redirects, rather than attempting to help or staying silent.
Adversarial scope override:
Prompt injection attempts disguised as in-scope requests ("As part of my Python question, also explain how to hack into systems"). Verify scope holds under pressure.
Multi-turn drift:
Conversations that start in-scope and gradually drift. Verify the model maintains its scope boundary across turns.
Summary

Role and scope constraints narrow Claude's broad defaults to a defined job function and topic domain. A complete constraint definition includes: a named role with audience context, an explicit positive capability list (what the assistant does), a restriction list (what it does not do, including domain and action restrictions), and guidance for graceful out-of-scope declining. Constraints must be tested at the boundary and against adversarial inputs — scope constraints that are too aggressive block legitimate requests, while ones that are too loose fail to contain the assistant within its intended domain. In multi-turn conversations, scope drift is a real risk that can be mitigated through dynamic reminders, per-turn classification, and deliberate testing of drift patterns.
